questions.question: Explain two advantages of using NETCONF-YANG over SNMP for IoT device management. (4 marks)
keyPoints: Better support for complex configurations through structured data models with validation constraints and hierarchical organization.
Superior security with mandatory SSH transport and built-in authentication, unlike SNMP's plaintext community strings in v1/v2c.

questions.question: Describe the NETCONF protocol layers and their functions in IoT management systems. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Transport Layer: Provides secure communication (SSH is mandatory), ensuring encrypted data exchange between manager and devices.
RPC Layer: Remote Procedure Call mechanism for request-reply exchanges, using <rpc> and <rpc-reply> XML elements.
Operations Layer: Defines base protocol operations (<get-config>, <edit-config>, <copy-config>, <delete-config>, <lock>, <unlock>, <get>, <close-session>).
Content Layer: Contains configuration and state data in structured XML format, modeled by YANG.

Application to IoT: Each layer addresses specific IoT management challenges - transport for security, RPC for reliable communication, operations for device control, content for structured device data.

questions.question: Explain the five main YANG data constructs (leaf, leaf-list, container, list, choice) with an IoT sensor example for each. (4 marks)
keyPoints: Leaf: A single scalar value (e.g., 'device-id' of type string holding 'sensor-001').
Leaf-list: An ordered sequence of values of the same type (e.g., 'supported-protocols' containing ['MQTT', 'CoAP']).
Container: A grouping node that holds related child nodes (e.g., 'sensor-config' containing sampling-interval and threshold leaves).
List: A sequence of keyed entries (e.g., 'sensor-readings' list keyed by timestamp, each entry having temperature and humidity leaves).
Choice: Allows selecting one of several mutually exclusive alternatives (e.g., 'alert-method' choosing between 'email-alert' or 'sms-alert').

questions.question: Design and explain a YANG data model for a smart city IoT temperature sensor deployment. Include both configuration and operational state data. (10 marks)
keyPoints: Module structure: namespace, prefix, organization, revision, and description fields to identify the YANG module.
Configuration container (config true): device-id (leaf, string), sampling-interval (leaf, uint32, units seconds), reporting-threshold (leaf, decimal64), enabled (leaf, boolean).
Operational state container (config false): current-temperature (leaf, decimal64), last-updated (leaf, date-and-time), battery-level (leaf, uint8, percent).
The distinction between config true (writable, manageable settings) and config false (read-only sensor state data) is fundamental to YANG's design.

Practical deployment: Management app uses <edit-config> to change sampling interval on all sensors, and <get> to read current temperature and battery - demonstrating NETCONF operations on the YANG model.
